overview: Generates an output file containing a list of possible terms found in the source content of processed text units. The step processes **all input documents** before creating the candidate term list, passing filter events through unchanged to the next step. Terms are found using three methods: Terminology annotations, Text Analysis annotations, or simple statistical analysis on groups of tokens. Non-translatable entries are skipped.

outputPath.help: Full path of the tab-delimited output file. The file is always written in **UTF-8** with one candidate term per line: occurrence count in the first column, term text in the second column (tab-separated).
outputPath.values: Supports the `${rootDir}` variable for the root directory of the input.
examples: `${rootDir}/terms.txt` — writes to the input root directory

autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the result file in the system default application after the pipeline completes.

sortByOccurrence.help: Controls the sort order of the output file. Results are sorted by occurrence count (descending) when enabled, or **alphabetically** when disabled.

useTerminologyAnnotations.help: Includes terms defined via the **Terminology annotation** (`TermsAnnotation`). These annotations are created by filters or other steps — for example, by [ITS Terminology data category](http://www.w3.org/TR/its/#terminology) rules when using the XML Filter.
notes: Requires upstream filters or steps that produce `TermsAnnotation` on text unit sources.

useTextAnalysisAnnotations.help: Includes spans annotated with the **Text Analysis annotation** on source content. These annotations are created, for example, by the Enrycher Step.
notes: Requires upstream steps (e.g., Enrycher Step) that produce Text Analysis annotations on source content spans.

useStatistics.help:
Finds term candidates using simple statistical analysis on groups of tokens (word sequences). A "term" is a sequence of words matching the configured min/max word count and minimum occurrence threshold.

This method does **not** apply any linguistic processing — words are not reduced to their [stems](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem).
notes: No stemming or lemmatization is applied — `running` and `run` are treated as different words.

minWordsPerTerm.help: The minimum number of words a token sequence must contain to be considered a candidate term.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

maxWordsPerTerm.help: The maximum number of words a token sequence can contain. Keep this to a reasonable maximum (**6 or 7, or less**) to avoid combinatorial explosion and noisy results.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

minOccurrences.help: The minimum number of times a word sequence must appear across **all input files** to be retained as a candidate term.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

keepCase.help: Controls whether case differences are preserved when computing token sequences. When disabled, `Term` and `term` are treated as the same word. When enabled, they are treated as distinct words.
examples: Disabled: `Term` and `term` → same word, merged counts
Enabled: `Term` and `term` → different words, separate counts

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

removeSubTerms.help:
Performs an extra post-processing pass that removes candidate terms that appear to be sub-sequences of longer candidate terms. The occurrence count is adjusted: a sub-term is only removed if all its occurrences can be accounted for as parts of longer terms.

For example, given:
- `gnu free` = 6
- `gnu free document` = 6
- `gnu free document license` = 5

The result is:
- `gnu free document` = 1 (retained — occurs once on its own)
- `gnu free document license` = 5 (retained)
- `gnu free` is removed (6 = 5 + 1, fully accounted for as sub-sequence)
notes: **Warning:** In some cases this may remove valid entries when they happen to only occur as sub-sequences of longer, less-valid entries.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

stopWordsPath.help: Path to a text file containing **stop words** — words that break a token sequence even if the maximum word count has not been reached. Leave empty to use the built-in English default list.
stopWordsPath.values: Plain text file, one word per line. The default English lists are embedded in the step's JAR at `net/sf/okapi/steps/termextraction`.
notes: The default lists are for English only. For other languages, you must provide custom word lists.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

notStartWordsPath.help: Path to a text file containing **not-start words** — words that cannot appear at the beginning of a term (but can appear within or at the end). Leave empty to use the built-in English default list.
notStartWordsPath.values: Plain text file, one word per line.
notes: The default lists are for English only. For other languages, you must provide custom word lists.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

notEndWordsPath.help: Path to a text file containing **not-end words** — words that cannot appear at the end of a term (but can appear within or at the beginning). Leave empty to use the built-in English default list.
notEndWordsPath.values: Plain text file, one word per line.
notes: The default lists are for English only. For other languages, you must provide custom word lists.

dependsOn.condition: only used when tokens-grouping statistics is enabled

processingNotes: The step collects data across **all input documents** before producing the output file — it does not output per-document results.
Filter events pass through unchanged to the next pipeline step.
Non-translatable entries are excluded from term extraction.
The output file is always written in **UTF-8** with tab-delimited columns (occurrence count, term).
The three extraction methods (Terminology annotations, Text Analysis annotations, token statistics) can be used independently or combined — each adds candidates to the same output list.
limitations: The built-in stop words, not-start words, and not-end words lists are **English only** — custom lists must be provided for other languages.
The statistical (tokens-grouping) method performs no linguistic processing — no stemming, lemmatization, or morphological analysis is applied.
The `removeSubTerms` option may incorrectly remove valid short terms that happen to only occur within longer sequences.

examples.title: Sub-term removal behavior
examples.description: Demonstrates how `removeSubTerms` adjusts occurrence counts and removes terms that are fully accounted for as sub-sequences of longer terms.
examples.input:
```
6	gnu free
6	gnu free document
5	gnu free document license
```
examples.output:
```
1	gnu free document
5	gnu free document license
```

`gnu free` is removed (6 occurrences = 5 in "gnu free document license" + 1 in "gnu free document"). `gnu free document` is kept with count 1 (it stands alone once beyond its appearances in the longer term).
